_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Bladen County, NC. Record survival for one of the oldest counties in the Lower Cape Fear, formed 1734, with generally good preservation from an early date.
display_name: Bladen County, North Carolina — Courthouse Atlas

previous_courthouses.location: Various locations; county seat not fixed until Elizabethtown established
previous_courthouses.fate: Early colonial court met at various locations along the Cape Fear. Records from this period are fragmentary.

previous_courthouses.location: Elizabethtown, NC — on the river bluff
previous_courthouses.fate: Served through the Civil War and into the early 20th century. Not destroyed by fire or war but outgrown.

record_losses.cause: Fragmentation and loss of early colonial records due to unstable governance
record_losses.description: Bladen County's earliest records are fragmentary. The county was enormous when formed in 1734 — covering much of what is now southeastern NC — and its court met irregularly at various locations before Elizabethtown was established. Early records were kept by individual officials and not always transferred when officials changed. Some colonial-era records survive at the NC State Archives rather than in the county courthouse.
record_losses.narrative_impact: Bladen County has some of the oldest records in the Lower Cape Fear, but they are incomplete for the colonial period. If {ancestor_name} lived here before 1770, records may exist — but they require searching both the county courthouse and the state archives.
record_losses.source: NC State Archives. Guide to County Records in the North Carolina State Archives. Raleigh: Division of Archives and History, 2001.

deeds.completeness: Fair for colonial era, good from 1770 forward. Some of the earliest deed records in the Cape Fear region.
deeds.repository: Bladen County Register of Deeds, Elizabethtown; NC State Archives microfilm

wills_estates.completeness: Fair — wills and estate records from the colonial period survive. Bladen's estate inventories from the antebellum period document both slaveholding and non-slaveholding families.
wills_estates.gaps: Some colonial-era gaps. Better coverage from 1780 forward.
wills_estates.repository: Bladen County Clerk of Court; NC State Archives

marriages.completeness: Fair — marriage bonds from the early national period. Some colonial-era marriages recorded in church registers.
marriages.gaps: Pre-1780 marriages must be sought in church records or colonial-era court entries.
marriages.repository: Bladen County Register of Deeds; NC State Archives microfilm

court_minutes.completeness: Fair — some pre-1770 fragments exist. Continuous from 1770.
court_minutes.gaps: Colonial-era minutes are thin. Revolution-era gaps.
court_minutes.repository: NC State Archives; Bladen County Clerk of Court

vital_records.completeness: Spotty until 1920s. Very rural county with dispersed population.
vital_records.repository: NC Office of Vital Records; NC State Archives

substitute_sources.description: Bladen County was one of the original Cape Fear counties, and its residents appear frequently in the colonial records — land grants, council proceedings, militia rosters, and correspondence.
substitute_sources.repository: NC State Archives; published volumes (Saunders and Clark editions)
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for colonial-era families. Land grants and military records name individuals who may appear in no surviving county record.
substitute_sources.source: Saunders, William L., ed. The Colonial Records of North Carolina. 10 vols. Raleigh, 1886-1890.

substitute_sources.description: Population schedules 1790-1940. Bladen County is enumerated from the first federal census. Agricultural and slave schedules (1850-1860) add significant economic detail for this agricultural county.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Standard census research. Slave schedules are critical for identifying enslaved populations on Bladen County plantations.
substitute_sources.source: National Archives and Records Administration. Federal Census Schedules, Bladen County, NC.

substitute_sources.description: Bladen County's churches — including some of the oldest Baptist congregations in the Cape Fear — maintained membership rolls and minutes. Bladen's Baptist churches affiliated with the Cape Fear Association and later associations whose minutes name individual members.
substitute_sources.repository: NC Baptist Historical Collection, Wake Forest University; individual congregations; NC State Archives
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Church records supplement the fragmentary civil records for the colonial and early national periods. Disciplinary minutes provide family and community detail found nowhere else.
substitute_sources.source: Paschal, George W. History of North Carolina Baptists. Raleigh: General Board of NC Baptist State Convention, 1930.

narrative_hooks: Bladen County's records go back to 1734 — among the oldest in the Lower Cape Fear. If {ancestor_name} held land here in the colonial period, the deed may still be on file in Elizabethtown, though it may take some searching through fragmentary early volumes.
The courthouse at Elizabethtown survived the Civil War without significant damage — {ancestor_name}'s deed, will, or marriage record from the 19th century is almost certainly intact.

Before 1913, no birth or death records were required, and in a county as rural and isolated as Bladen, even church records are sparse. The census may be the only proof that {ancestor_name} existed between deed transactions.

Bladen County was enormous when it was formed in 1734 — it covered what later became Columbus, Robeson, and parts of other counties. If {ancestor_name}'s land seems to be in Bladen but you cannot find the deed, check whether the property ended up in a daughter county when Bladen was subdivided.
source_references: NC State Archives. Guide to County Records in the North Carolina State Archives. Raleigh: Division of Archives and History, 2001.
Saunders, William L., ed. The Colonial Records of North Carolina. 10 vols. Raleigh, 1886-1890.
Paschal, George W. History of North Carolina Baptists. Raleigh: General Board of NC Baptist State Convention, 1930.
Leary, Helen F. M., ed. North Carolina Research: Genealogy and Local History. 2nd ed. Raleigh: North Carolina Genealogical Society, 1996.
